next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

The pool was empty except for Lena.

Margot settled into the coaching chair on the observation deck and woke the session display. It was six forty-five a.m. The facility hadn't come online yet. The sprinters would not start until eight. The calibration bays were dark. Below the glass, the pool lay flat under the overheads, ten lanes of hard blue light. Lena stood on the far deck pulling on her cap. When she tucked her hair back, the port at the base of her skull showed for a second: a small round disc, skin-colored, then hidden again.

Before eight, the deck console ran in local mode. It talked to the lane hardware, the athlete's link, and whatever coaching unit was physically paired in the room. Anything medical serious enough to finish its countdown would queue itself for central routing and fire the moment the campus network woke. Until then there was only the room, the water, and whoever had a thumb on the controls.

Margot strapped the feed interface to her right wrist. The pads found her pulse and warmed. Her unit was personal, not institute-issued. It mirrored raw sensory packets by default because coaches were supposed to review them after hard sets, annotate them, dock the unit later, and let the central system keep only what the export profile allowed. She entered the athlete ID. The set was standard aerobic. Pace target 1:04 per hundred. Easy enough on paper. Clean enough to live inside.

She connected.

Air came first. The deck was cool on Lena's bare feet. There was tightness through the shoulders from yesterday. There was also a dry ache behind t...

prev_tail_excerpt:
...said. "If your system handshake settles clean, light work tomorrow."

He said it as if there wasn't a person's nervous system on the other side of the sentence.

The German javelin thrower took his appointment card and tucked it straight into his accreditation lanyard. The whispering athlete near the back asked for the latest available slot, then took what he was given without argument when the answer came back, "Tomorrow morning."

Tomas stayed seated long enough that Kai thought he might refuse the next step after all. Then he got up, gave his binder to the gloved man, and stood in the short line with the others, shoulders squared the way athletes stood for accreditation photos. When he came back past Kai's row he had a white appointment card between two fingers and would not quite meet Kai's eyes.

Kai's own binder lay open on his lap. At some point he had opened it without noticing. The signature line was blank. Behind it waited the addenda Tomas had touched: sponsorship compliance, support obligations, data-use schedules, calibration consent. His name sat there in print, neat and harmless-looking, with a box on the next page for preferred installation time.

When the gloved hand reached for another signed binder, his left wrist answered with a deep throb inside the brace. He closed his own and pressed his good hand flat over the cover until the pulse eased.

Outside the conference room windows, the Alps stood white under a sky that was starting to clear. Wind peeled snow off the high ridges in thin sheets, something immense and indifferent moving just beyond the glass.

An alternative path exists but is uncertain, and the virus's origin suggests corporate engineering
